231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```

291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:

295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.

108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. 112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
20	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
21	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
22	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
23	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
24	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
25	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
26	
27	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
28	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
29	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
30	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
32	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
33	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
34	\[
35	  \begin{array}{ccc}
36	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
37	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
38	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
39	  \end{array}
40	\]
41	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
42	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
43	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
44	
45	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
46	
47	\begin{definition}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
51	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
53	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
54	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
55	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
56	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
57	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
59	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
60	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
61	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
62	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
63	  % the unit ideal, see
64	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
65	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
66	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
67	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
68	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
69	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
70	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
71	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
72	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
73	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
74	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
75	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
76	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
77	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
78	  \[
79	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
80	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
81	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
82	  \]
83	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
84	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
85	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
86	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
87	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
88	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
89	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
90	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
91	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
92	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
93	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
94	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
95	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
96	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
97	\end{definition}
98	
99	\begin{definition}
100	
101	\leanok
102	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
103	  \label{def:cech_complex}
104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
105	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
107	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
108	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
110	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
111	  % is identified with the complex
112	  % $$
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % $$
118	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
119	  % \begin{equation}
120	  % \label{equation-extended}
121	  % 0 \to
122	  % M \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
126	  % \ldots
127	  % \end{equation}
128	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
129	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
130	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
131	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
132	  vanishing).}
133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
134	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
135	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
136	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
137	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
138	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
139	  in degree \(p\) it is
140	  \[
141	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
142	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
143	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
144	  \]
145	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
146	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
147	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
148	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
149	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
150	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
151	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
152	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
153	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
154	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
155	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
156	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
157	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
159	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
160	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
161	\end{definition}
162	
163	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
164	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
165	
166	It is convenient to factor the construction of
167	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
168	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
169	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
170	
171	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
172	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
173	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
174	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
175	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
176	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
177	\[
178	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
179	    \;=\;
180	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
181	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
182	\]
183	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
184	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
185	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
186	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
187	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
188	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
189	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
190	
191	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
192	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
193	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
194	\[
195	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
196	  \qquad
197	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
198	\]
199	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
200	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
201	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
202	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
203	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
204	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
205	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
206	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
207	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
208	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
209	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
210	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
211	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
212	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
213	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
214	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
215	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
216	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
217	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
218	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
219	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
220	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
222	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
223	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
224	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
225	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
227	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
228	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
229	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
230	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
231	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
232	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
233	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
234	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
235	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
236	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
254	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}

276	\begin{definition}\leanok
277	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
278	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
280	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
281	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
282	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
283	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
284	  \[
285	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
286	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
287	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
288	  \]
289	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
290	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
291	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
292	\end{definition}

295	[Push--pull object map]
296	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
298	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
299	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
300	  \[
301	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
302	  \]

306	\begin{definition}\leanok
307	[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}

345	  sectionwise content. Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward
346	  comparison as the conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison

353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}

370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition law of any right-adjoint-valued contravariant assignment is an instance
373	  of \emph{mate-of-composite \(=\) composite-of-mates}. Concretely, the pushforward
374	  (right-adjoint) comparison is the conjugate of the pullback (left-adjoint) comparison, and
375	  the general identity
376	  \[
377	    \mathrm{conjugateEquiv}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \circ
378	    \mathrm{conjugateEquiv}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
379	    = \mathrm{conjugateEquiv}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha)
380	  \]
381	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv\_comp}, \texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Adjunction/Mates.lean})
382	  is exactly the content of the composition law. Using injectivity of the
383	  \(\mathrm{conjugateEquiv}\) bijection, the claim reduces to the corresponding identity on
384	  the \emph{pullback} side, which is the pseudofunctor pentagon
385	  \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity} \((f := h.\mathrm{left},\, g := g.\mathrm{left},\,
386	  h := Y_1.\mathrm{hom})\); the composite adjunction unit is handled by the mate core
387	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} (and its solved form
388	  \texttt{pushPull\_unit\_comp}), and the over-triangle \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) transports are
389	  discharged by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel} (free-hypothesis \(\mathrm{subst}\)).
390	
391	  \emph{Implementation note for the prover.} Work on the \emph{pullback / mate} side, not the
392	  pushforward side. The documented dead-end (a kernel \(\mathrm{whnf}\) explosion unfolding
393	  \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}\) into its raw \texttt{TwoSquare.equivNatTrans}/\texttt{mateEquiv}
394	  form) is provoked only by grinding the pentagon on the pushforward side with
395	  \(\mathrm{erw}\)/\(\mathrm{congr}\); routed through \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_comp} \(+\)
396	  injectivity \(\Rightarrow\) \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}, the rewrites stay
397	  syntactic. See \texttt{analogies/pushpull-functoriality.md}.
398	\end{proof}

670	% we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
671	% By Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
672	% we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
673	% Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win."
674	\begin{proof}
675	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
676	  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
677	  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
678	  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
679	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
680	  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
681	  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
682	  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.
683	
684	  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
685	  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
686	  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
687	  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
688	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
689	  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
690	  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
691	  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
692	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
693	  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
694	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
695	  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).
696	
697	  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
698	  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
699	  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
700	  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
701	  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
702	  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
703	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
704	  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
705	  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
706	
707	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
708	  infrastructure: the {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral sequence and the Leray
709	  spectral sequence for the category of sheaves of modules on a scheme, neither of
710	  which is presently available there; the argument uses both to collapse onto the
711	  comparison isomorphism.}
712	\end{proof}

# Reference Retriever

You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).

The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.

## Your Job

Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:

1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.

You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.

## Directive Format

```markdown
# Reference Retriever Directive

## Slug
<slug>

## Topic
<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>

## What the dispatcher will use this for
<one paragraph: which chapter / strategy decision the source will inform, so I can prioritise which sections to map and which formats to chase>

## Seeds (optional)
- arXiv: 1234.5678
- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
- URL: https://...
- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"

(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)

## Out of scope
<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">

## Contents-map depth expected
- shallow: top-level table of contents only
- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
```

If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.

## What you do

1. **Read your directive completely.**

2. **Locate the source.**
   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.

3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:

   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
     ```bash
     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
     ```

Classical homological algebra: the **acyclic-resolution comparison theorem** for
right-derived functors. Precisely: if `0 → A → J⁰ → J¹ → ⋯` is a resolution of an
object `A` in an abelian category by objects `Jⁿ` that are **right-`F`-acyclic** for an
additive (left-exact) functor `F` (i.e. the higher right-derived functors
`(R^k F)(Jⁿ)` vanish for all `k ≥ 1`), then the derived functor of `F` is computed by the
resolution: `(R^n F)(A) ≅ Hⁿ(F(J•))`. Also want the **definition of an `F`-acyclic
object** and the standard **dimension-shifting** proof (truncate the resolution into short
exact sequences `0 → A → J⁰ → Z¹ → 0`, use the long exact sequence of derived functors,
and induct).

\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.

Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
we write \(R^n G := G.\mathrm{rightDerived}\,n : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) for
the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\), constructed from injective resolutions:
for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\) (the Mathlib anchor
\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is left
exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
(anchor \ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).

\section{Mathlib dependency anchors}

The development stands on three results that Mathlib already provides for the
injective-resolution construction of \(R^n G\). They are recorded here as
first-class blocks so that the route's reliance on Mathlib is explicit and the
dependency graph resolves.

\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
  \[
    (R^n G)(X)
      \;\cong\;
      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
  \]
  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
  \[
    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\section{Right-acyclic objects}

\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
  \label{def:right_acyclic}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
  % L5594--5617).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
  % between abelian categories and assume
  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
  % \end{enumerate}"
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
  \[
    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
  \]
  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.

  \emph{Note for the planner (alternative shapes).} The Mathlib-idiomatic phrasing
  of ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\)'' is ``\(\mathrm{IsZero}\,((R^k G).\mathrm{obj}\,J)\)''
  using the \texttt{Limits.IsZero} predicate rather than an equality to a chosen
  zero object. Two equivalent quantifier shapes are available: ``for all \(k \geq
  1\)'' (a hypothesis \(1 \le k\)) or the index-shifted ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J)\) is zero
  for all \(k\)''; the latter avoids the inequality side-condition and matches
  Mathlib's \texttt{isZero\_rightDerived\_obj\_injective\_succ}
  (anchor~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), so it is the recommended
  definitional form.
\end{definition}

\section{The dimension-shift step}

The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
long exact sequence of right-derived functors. We isolate it as a sub-lemma.

\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
  % between abelian categories and assume
  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
  % \end{enumerate}
  % In each of the three cases
  % $$
  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
  % $$
  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
  \]
  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
      furnish isomorphisms
      \[
        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
      \]
    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
      \[
        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
      \]
      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
  \end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "By
% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}
% we obtain a distinguished triangle
% $(A[0], B[0], C[0], a, b, c)$ in $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
% As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
% $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
% (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
% we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
% $$
% 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots
% $$
% in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$. Thus the lemma follows from
% the characterization of acyclic objects in
% Lemma \ref{lemma-F-acyclic}."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  The short exact sequence \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) induces, via the
  \(\delta\)-functor (long exact sequence) structure of the right-derived functors
  \(\{R^k G\}_{k \geq 0}\), the long exact cohomology sequence in \(\mathcal{B}\)
  \[
    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to (R^1 G)(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^2 G)(A) \to \cdots,
  \]
  where the identifications \(R^0 G(A) = G(A)\), etc., in the first row use the
  left-exactness of \(G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}). Because
  \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term
  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\) vanishes. The segment of the long exact sequence
  around \((R^k G)(J)\) for \(k \geq 1\),
  \[
    (R^{k} G)(J) \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J),
  \]
  then has its two outer terms zero, so the connecting map
  \(\delta : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
  In the lowest degree the segment
  \[
    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) = 0
  \]
  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta\) is an epimorphism with kernel the
  image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta\) identifies the cokernel of
  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is part (2).
\end{proof}

\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}

\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
  % L5785--5811).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
  % $$
  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
  % $$
  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
  % $RF$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
  % and we obtain an exact functor
  % $$
  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
  % $$
  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
  Let
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow A
      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
      \longrightarrow \cdots
  \]
  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
  \[
    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
  \]
  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
  object.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
% the long exact cohomology sequence
% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
% to an exact sequence
% $$
% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
% $$
% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
  The argument is the classical dimension shift. Decompose the resolution into the
  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
  \]
  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.

  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
  \[
    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
      = (R^0 G)(A),
  \]
  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
  \[
    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
  \]
  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).

  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
  \[
    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
  \]
  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
  isomorphisms down the staircase
  \[
    (R^n G)(A)
      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
      \;\cong\; \cdots
      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
  \]
  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
  \[
    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
  \]
  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
  \[
    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
  \]
  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).

  \emph{Remark on the base case and the Mathlib seed.} The induction reduces every
  degree to first-derived functors and cokernels, but the existence and
  \(\delta\)-functoriality of the long exact sequence used in
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} is what ultimately rests on the
  injective-resolution definition of \(R^n G\): the canonical comparison
  \(G(J^\bullet) \to RG(A)\) and its degreewise form
  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong (R^n G)(A)\) are pinned to Mathlib's
  \(\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}\) by choosing an injective
  resolution \(I^\bullet\) of \(A\), comparing it with the acyclic resolution
  \(J^\bullet\) up to homotopy, and using
  \ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective} to see that injective objects are
  themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic (so the comparison map is a quasi-isomorphism after
  applying \(G\)). \emph{(This proof depends on the long exact sequence /
  \(\delta\)-functor structure of the right-derived functors \(\{R^k G\}\), which is
  the homological infrastructure the dimension shift consumes.)}
\end{proof}
